name: Northern Route: Laredo and Colindres Loop
name_original: Paseo Maríteo, Arroya El Regatón y Laredo – circular desde Liendo
description: Cycle the Maritime Walk, El Regatón Stream, and Laredo on this 15.2-mile touring route through Cantabria's diverse landscapes.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Liendo offers routes through a diverse landscape in Cantabria, Spain. The region features a blend of coastal paths, green valleys, and rolling hills. Cyclists can experience routes that combine views of the Cantabrian Sea with inland terrain. The area's varied topography provides options for different cycling preferences. Best touring cycling routes around Liendo The most popular touring cycling route is Northern Route: Laredo and Colindres Loop, a 15.2 miles (24.5 km) trail that takes 1 hour 40 minutes to complete. This route offers coastal views and passes through Laredo and Colindres. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Bosquerato de Liondo – Puebla Vieja de Laredo loop from Oriñón, a difficult 15.6 miles (25.2 km) path. This route features significant elevation gain through varied terrain. Local touring cyclists also love the Oriñón Bay – Playa de Oriñón loop from Liendo, a 14.6 miles (23.4 km) trail leading through coastal areas and inland hills, often completed in about 1 hour 36 minutes. Touring cycling around Liendo is defined by coastal paths, green valleys, and varied elevation. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to more challenging routes. The routes in Liendo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 350 reviews. More than 2900 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Liendo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Liendo?
A: Liendo offers a wide selection of touring cycling routes, with over 130 options available. These routes cater to various skill levels, including 23 easy, 34 moderate, and 75 difficult trails.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Liendo?
A: The region of Cantabria, where Liendo is located, generally offers pleasant conditions for cycling during spring and autumn. These seasons typically provide milder temperatures and less extreme weather, making them ideal for exploring both coastal paths and inland terrain.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Liendo?
A: The touring cycling routes in Liendo are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average rating of 4.5 stars from over 350 reviews. More than 2,900 touring cyclists have explored the area using komoot, often praising the diverse landscapes that blend coastal views with green valleys and rolling hills.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes for beginners in Liendo?
A: Yes, Liendo offers several options suitable for beginners. There are 23 easy touring cycling routes, many of which might include sections of the region's greenways or less challenging coastal paths, providing a gentle introduction to the area's beautiful scenery.

Q: Are there challenging touring cycling routes in Liendo?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Liendo provides 75 difficult touring cycling routes. These often feature significant elevation gains and varied terrain, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40337055" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bosquerato de Liondo – Puebla Vieja de Laredo loop from Oriñón&lt;/a&gt;, which includes substantial climbing through its 15.6 miles (25.2 km).

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Liendo?
A: Touring cycling in Liendo offers a diverse range of terrain. You can expect routes that combine stunning coastal paths with views of the Cantabrian Sea, green valleys, and rolling hills. Some routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012450447/northern-route-laredo-and-colindres-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Northern Route: Laredo and Colindres Loop&lt;/a&gt;, offer a mix of coastal and urban landscapes, while others venture into more varied inland topography.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Liendo?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in Liendo are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012450447/northern-route-laredo-and-colindres-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Northern Route: Laredo and Colindres Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40332679" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oriñón Bay – Playa de Oriñón loop from Liendo&lt;/a&gt;, which covers coastal areas and inland hills.

Q: What natural landmarks can I see while cycling around Liendo?
A: While cycling around Liendo, you can encounter several natural landmarks. Notable features include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1834895" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Devil's Eye&lt;/a&gt;, a viewpoint offering panoramic views through a natural rock window, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2422215" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Regatón Stream&lt;/a&gt;. You might also spot the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4416895" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lone Tree on the Way to Ojos del Diablo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical sites or castles to visit along the touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, the area around Liendo is rich in history. Cyclists can explore sites such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5411028" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puebla Vieja de Laredo&lt;/a&gt;, a historic old town, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6656175" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santa Ana Castle and Lighthouse&lt;/a&gt;. Other historical points of interest include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5156845" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuerte de San Carlos&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5322892" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruins of Montehano Castle&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling routes in Liendo?
A: Liendo offers a selection of easy routes that are suitable for families. While specific 'family-friendly' designations aren't always explicit, routes with lower difficulty grades and less elevation, potentially utilizing former railway lines converted into greenways, are generally good choices for a family outing.

Q: Is Liendo suitable for dog-friendly touring cycling?
A: Many outdoor activities in the Liendo region are generally dog-friendly, especially on less crowded paths. However, it's always advisable to keep dogs on a leash, especially in nature reserves or near livestock, and to check specific route regulations or local signage regarding pets.

Q: Where can I find parking for touring cycling routes in Liendo?
A: While specific parking areas for each route are not detailed, you can typically find parking in or near the main towns and villages that serve as starting points for routes, such as Liendo itself, Laredo, or Oriñón. Look for public parking lots or designated areas near trailheads.

Q: Can I use public transport to access touring cycling routes in Liendo?
A: Public transport options in Cantabria may allow for bicycle transport, though policies can vary by service and time of year. It's recommended to check with local bus or train operators in advance regarding their specific rules for carrying bicycles to plan your access to Liendo's cycling routes.
tips: At this point you have a great view of the Bay of Laredo and the Iglesia de Santa María to the north from a small "balcony" along the main road. A few rudimentary stone benches invite you to enjoy this view a little longer and undisturbed by traffic.
Worth a visit is the decorative El Tunnel, which leads from the old town to a seaside viewpoint.

A long, wide and beautiful promenade for cycling. It is right next to the ocean and with excellent benches and views. It is almost deserted in winter but in summer they set up a market full of products.

The dune system here is one of the most important on the Atlantic coast. It is integrated into the Natural Park of the Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes, an important wetland. Migratory birds visit the area during the fall and winter months and if you go with binoculars, you can see mallards, herons and egrets in their natural environment. There are two campsites in the area.

faqs.question: What is the overall difficulty of this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is officially rated as moderate for touring bicycles. While much of it is paved, be prepared for some unpaved sections and potentially soft sand, especially near El Regatón Beach. Cyclists with loaded bikes might find certain parts challenging, and some sources suggest it could require pushing your bike in segments.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the trail?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features paved surfaces, ideal for touring cycling. However, you will encounter unpaved segments, particularly around El Regatón Stream and its beach, which has fine, golden sand and dunes. The Laredo Promenade offers a long, wide, and beautifully paved section.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this circular route?
faqs.answer: For this circular route starting from Liendo, you can look for parking options within the town of Liendo itself. Alternatively, Laredo, a larger town along the route, also offers various parking facilities, which could be a convenient starting point if you prefer.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While specific regulations for dogs on this exact route are not detailed, generally, trails in natural parks like the Parque Natural de las Marismas de Santoña, Victoria y Joyel require dogs to be kept on a leash to protect wildlife, especially migratory birds. Always ensure your dog is under control.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally during the spring and autumn months when the weather is milder. If you are interested in birdwatching, the fall and winter months are ideal for spotting migratory birds like mallards, herons, and egrets in the Natural Park of the Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay an entrance fee for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. The trail passes through public areas and parts of the Parque Natural de las Marismas de Santoña, Victoria y Joyel, which is freely accessible.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter diverse scenery and notable spots. Highlights include the coastal views from the Maritime Walk, the ecological richness of El Regatón Stream and its dune system, and the vibrant Laredo Promenade. You can also enjoy panoramic views from Mirador del Alto de Laredo.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: The route passes through the Natural Park of the Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes, an important wetland. This area is excellent for birdwatching, especially during fall and winter, when you might spot migratory birds such as mallards, herons, and egrets.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers significant sections of the Northern Route (Cantabria) and the Senda del Litoral. You'll also find it briefly joins parts of the Ruta de Carlos V and various segments of the Paseo Marítimo.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through Laredo, a coastal town, you will find numerous cafes, restaurants, and pubs, particularly along the Laredo Promenade and in its urban areas. Liendo also offers some local establishments.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a touring bicycle ride on this trail?
faqs.answer: Given the varied terrain, including potential sandy sections, it's advisable to bring a touring bicycle suitable for both paved and unpaved surfaces. Essential items include water, snacks, sun protection, a repair kit, and appropriate clothing for changing weather. If you plan to birdwatch, binoculars would be a great addition.

waypoints.name: The Decorated Tunnel of Castro Urdiales
